titleStmt.title: Letter from Michael O'Riordan to Bishop E. T. O'Dwyer, 12 May 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Michael O'Riordan

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This letter was written by Monsignor Michael O'Riordan (1857-1919), rector at the Irish College in Rome, to Bishop Edward Thomas O'Dwyer (1842-1917), who served as the Bishop of Limerick from 1888 until his death. This letter concerns O’Riordan’s reaction to the Easter Rising. O’Riordan is critical of the excessive British response to the Rising. Had the executive shown greater leniency the insurgents, who were to become immortal heroes, would probably have been instead regarded as thoughtless youths. He also refers to the death of Francis Sheehy Skeffington (1878 – 1916), an Irish, pacifist, writer and feminist who had adopted his wife Hanna Sheehy's surname. Skeffington was executed on the morning of 26 April 1916, along with two other civilians who were shot by firing squad on the orders of Captain John Bowen-Colthurst, an experienced officer of the Royal Irish Rifles. Colthurst would later be court-martialed by the British authorities for his actions. O' Riordan states that the execution was lawless and stupid.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Irish College Rome 12 May 1916 My Lord Bishop I enclose a receipt. I
suppose the Censor is at work
again. If he opens this letter he
will have an opportunity of
revising his Latin â if he ever
knew any. The Government Executive
seems to be as foolish as the
insurgents. Mr Skeffington never
had any sense. But he should
have had a trial; to shoot
him without one was
lawless and stupid. It will
probably be sought now to impress
on the public conviction that
all were executed without trial.
If the (executive) showed living under
their natural provocation the
the insurgents who are now likely to
become immortal heroes could
probably have lived in the
public memory of Ireland as
impulsive thoughtless youths the the scorn of wisdom there is
not a pin to choose between
the executive and the insurgents. We are still in the dark
here as to what directly led up
to the trouble. I take no notice
of what politicians & journalists
say on any side since the war
began. They have made lying an
art and a virtue â it is a
moral duty to lie if it pays
It is a literal truth that I have
not read one sentence about the
battle of Verdun and I seem to
know as much about it as those
who study it with maps â more
because I am aware of my
ignorance. I have heard that some persons
here have sent a telegram to Mr Redmond
on the trouble in Dublin as from
'the Irish in Rome'. I know nothing of it
and the Augustinians & Franciscans know
nothing of it. I am told that the
sender of it (who the were I
cannot say) was born in Scotland. So
we have a Foaley St in Rome as in
London and there are traitors in it
too. It is a foolish thing for nearly
all the Irish in Rome are students or
nuns. I am My Lord
yours obediently
M O'Riordan P S As I have these remarks on the troubles in Dublin I have
not put 'a priest sends sermon' on the envelope
